entry.title: Re: Combining SQL Services and CDS External Entities for modern ABAP-to-ABAP Data Federation
entry.content:
Hi @lstern ,

I saw this in the roadmap.

Using the Cloud Connector support for CDS external entities, it will be possible to call on premise- or private Cloud systems from within SAP BTP ABAP environment. This allows for SQL-based federation by invoking either ABAP SQL services or native HANA artifacts that are exposed in these on-premise oder private Cloud systems.

Does it also support calling a BTP ABAP system from within the S4 private cloud system?

entry.title: Re: Indeed’s Transition Away from XML Feeds: What SAP SuccessFactors Customers Need to Know

Our Indeed contacts did NOT inform us either - or at least not that this was a required move - they had been trying to sell us to move to this new integration of theirs, Indeed Apply. To be honest, we had thought we'd wait until we were on SmartRecruiters, tbd, and then plan this new integration at that time. entry.title: Manage Work Center Capacity App - Problem with adding shifts
entry.content:
Hi SAP Gurus,

I am currently facing a small problem with Best Practice 31L in our Public Cloud test system. In section 4.1.3.2 of the test script, it says that we should add another shift to increase the avaiable capacity. The standard capacity for that specific work centers runs from 7AM to 4PM everyday, with no shift definition. So as per script I am trying to add another shift that starts at 4PM till 11PM or so, but when also selecting Shift definition YS2, which is defined in customizing as starting from 2PM till 10PM, I get the error that the shifts are overlapping, even tho the first shift of the day ends at 4PM and my manually added shift would start at 4PM, so no overlap in that sense. Is the system ignoring my manually input starting time and is assuming the shift is starting at 2PM, because i selected YS2 as shift definition? When I try to add the shift with no shift definition selected, it works as it should, but with shift definition it gives the described error.

 

Thanks for your input

entry.title: Copying data from SAP to Excel
entry.content:
Hi,

I have an issue with copying SAP column data (e.g. from MB51 using a Flat List Layout) to Excel.

Using a previous SAP GUI version, I was able to select non-adjacent columns (e.g. 1st, 3rd, 5th) and paste them into Excel using Ctrv+V. The data would appear in columns A, B & C.

Using a new SAP GUI version, data is now pasted into column A, C & E. Columns B & E are blank.

Do I need to change something in the GUI interface in order for SAP to ignore the columns that are not selected for pasting?   

Thanks,

Wolfman.

entry.title: SAP’s Most Popular Certification Gets a Major Upgrade
entry.content:
March is officially the month of ABAP. SAP’s most popular certification for back-end developers, the Back-End Developer ABAP Cloud, is getting a major upgrade. At the same time, the SAP Community developer challenge for ABAP is going live, making this the perfect moment to sharpen your skills and validate your expertise.

 

A Reimagined Certification Experience

The ABAP Cloud certification exam has been redesigned. Moving beyond traditional question-based formats, the new exam introduces a performance-based, open-book experience.

Learners now work directly in authentic SAP systems, navigating AI-supported, real-world scenarios that reflect actual project work. The focus is no longer on memorization; it’s about demonstrating practical skills that matter on the job.

What does it mean for you?

If you’ve been preparing for certification through the SAP Learning site, you’re already familiar with hands-on exercises and scenario-based tasks embedded in the learning journeys. The new exam format simply extends that approach.

From the moment you begin your certification, the experience feels familiar because it mirrors how you’ve been learning all along.

Every practical exam, whether system-based or scenario-driven reflects the reality of SAP projects:

  • Timeboxed tasks performed in live SAP systems
  • Realistic customer and project scenarios
  • AI-supported simulations
  • Open-book access to resources such as SAP Help Portal, SAP Learning, SAP Community, and SAP Joule for Consultants (where applicable)
  • A clear emphasis on proving your ability to do the work

This shift represents something bigger: trust. Trust in learners who invest in building real skills and trust from employers who rely on SAP-certified professionals to deliver value from day one.

 

How to get certified as Backend Developer for ABAP Cloud

Earning your certification is a structured journey. Whether you are new to ABAP or looking to formalize years of experience, the following resources will help you build a strong foundation and deepen your expertise.

 

Recommended Courses to Build and Deepen Your Skills

Start with the fundamentals and progressively expand your knowledge:

  • Course: Learning Basic ABAP Programming
  • Course: Deepening Your ABAP Programming Knowledge
  • Course: Building Data Models with the ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services
  • Course: Practicing Clean Core Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • SAP Certification: Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud

 

Together, these courses prepare you not just to pass the exam but to succeed in real-world ABAP Cloud development. Learn more about the exam details in this community blog by @JenniferHauck.

 

Connect Live with SAP Experts and Strengthen Your Exam Readiness

Learning doesn’t have to be a solo experience. If you’re looking for direct interaction, practical tips, and expert guidance, join the upcoming live sessions available through your SAP Learning Hub subscription:

  • Getting Started with ABAP Cloud Development
  • Get Certified: SAP Certified – Back-End Developer ABAP Cloud
  • Stay Certified: Back-End Developer – ABAP Cloud

These sessions offer the opportunity to clarify questions, reinforce key concepts, and gain confidence before taking the exam.

 

Join the ABAP Developer Community to Learn, Share, and Grow

Certification is just one milestone in your journey. Continuous learning happens through collaboration.

Visit the ABAP developer forum here on the SAP Community to:

  • Read expert blogs
  • Ask technical questions
  • Share your own insights
  • Connect with developers worldwide

Being part of the community ensures you stay current and continue growing long after certification.

 

Experience Hands-On Learning at SAP CodeJam Events Near You

If you prefer immersive, in-person learning, don’t miss the upcoming SAP CodeJam events. These sessions are designed to provide practical, guided experience with real SAP technologies in a collaborative environment.

Upcoming sessions include:

  • April 7: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (São Paulo, Brazil)
  • April 8: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Londrina, Brazil)
  • April 23: ABAP Cloud and the RESTful Application Programming Model (Warsaw, Poland)

 

These events are an excellent opportunity to deepen your understanding, expand your network, and gain hands-on practice. Be sure to explore the full event calendar regularly, as new sessions are added on an ongoing basis.

 

Continue Your Learning with On-Demand Developer Content

Can’t attend live? Explore past Devtoberfest sessions, Tech Bytes, SAP CodeTalks, and more on the official SAP Developers YouTube channel

On-demand content allows you to learn at your own pace while staying connected to the latest innovations in ABAP and SAP development.

March is the perfect time to invest in your ABAP Cloud journey. With a certification experience designed to reflect real project work, you’re not just passing an exam, you’re demonstrating that you’re ready to build, extend, and innovate in the SAP ecosystem.

entry.title: Re: Combining SQL Services and CDS External Entities for modern ABAP-to-ABAP Data Federation
entry.content:
Hi @rammel ,

From a technical perspective, it is already possible to read data from a BTP ABAP system within an S/4 Private Cloud system. However, this requires the ODBC Driver for ABAP to be installed in the HANA instance used by the S/4 Private Cloud system. If you're interested, I recommend reaching out to your S/4 Private Cloud contact.

Best regards,
Leon

entry.title: Modelling IBP-to-IBP Integration Flows with SAP Integration Suite – Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hello Community,

 

Introduction

Over the past years, CI-DS has been widely used to model integration interfaces with SAP IBP as both source and target system with or without an intermediate step via storing data in a file on server, covering use cases such as:

  • Housecleaning – Marking obsolete master data to alter clean within IBP. While S/4HANA's integration add-on can cleanse its own staging tables, this doesn't cascade automatically to IBP.
  • Rule-based attribute value definition – The Manage Rules for Master Data Maintenance app in IBP has limitations, for complex cases the integration framework is usually the way to go.
  • Data copies with transformations – Handling cases where the standard IBP Copy operator falls short: copying a key figure with a period offset, duplicating data from one planning object to multiple target planning objects, etc.
  • Version and planning area fan-out – Sending master data or key figures from one version (or planning area) to multiple versions (or planning areas), including data transformations.
  • Complex key figure calculations not yet simplified by the IBP configuration layer.
  • Master data attributes as KF reloads – Initializing time buckets at the end of the rolling time horizon.

Since IBP release 2508, the Harmonised Planning Area (HPA) has introduced a unified structure supporting both tactical and operational planning, combining time-series and order-based data. Alongside this major innovation, the integration layer is also evolving: as highlighted in a previous blog entry, the integration framework for IBP is shifting toward a combination of RTI and CI (the Cloud Integration capability of the Integration Suite service on BTP).

This blog entry continues the series on highlighting how to model IBP-to-IBP integration flows using Cloud Integration.

 

How to Model IBP-to-IBP Flows in Cloud Integration

Integration Suite offers the flexibility to cover these scenarios — and many others — within a single, configurable integration flow.

The starting point is the "SAP IBP – Reusable Integration Flows Examples" package. This package provides two sets of grouped reusable iFlows : first to read and extract data from a source IBP destination, another to write data to the to the same or a different IBP destination. Before building your own flows, I strongly recommend consulting the Integration guide for SAP IBP reusable integration flows examples.

One way to model this end to end is shown below as an overview:

 

📥 Step 1 – Read Data from IBP

To read data from IBP, call the following reusable iFlows in sequence, using RequestReply building blocks with a ProcessDirect adapter:

#

iFlow

Purpose

1

SAP_IBP_Read_-_Initialize

Opens a server-side read session on the IBP OData service, allocating resources to hold query context, pagination state, and result set.

2

SAP_IBP_Read_-_Fetch_Data

Fetches data pages from IBP's OData service, handling pagination automatically until all records have been extracted.

3

SAP_IBP_Read_-_Close

Closes the read session opened by Initialize. You can call this at the end of your flow — including after the write steps — to release server resources.

⚠️ Note: the Close iFlow is intentionally called last, after data transformation and all write steps have completed.

 

🔄 Step 2 – Transform Data

Once the XML payload has been fetched from IBP, you can apply transformations using the Message Mapping building block. This is where IBP-to-IBP flows deliver real value: transforming or adjusting data in ways that would be difficult or impossible to achieve directly within IBP.

Example: Housecleaning via a Deletion Indicator

Consider a scenario where S/4HANA regularly sends products to IBP based on an integration add-on filter (e.g., MRP type = X0). If a product is later made non-IBP-relevant, the add-on can stop sending it — but because data is loaded in insert/update mode, the product record remains in IBP master data.

A clean solution: use a timestamp attribute that tracks the last update date for each master data record. In your Message Mapping, you can evaluate this date and — if the record hasn't been updated since yesterday — mark it with an X in a Deletion Indicator attribute. The logic relies on the fact that master data is loaded daily: a record not updated today was not sent by S/4HANA and is therefore no longer integration-relevant.

Here's how to build this mapping once you've added it in your flow:

Load your source and target XSD schemas (an example is attached to this blog entry — rename the file extension from .xml to .xsd). You can use two different schema files for source and target, adapting each to include only the fields you're reading from or writing back to IBP. An XML editor like Notepad++ works well for these adjustments.

 

Once you've loaded both schemas, you can use the drag and drop method to link your attributes together 1:1.
For our example, focusing on the deletion indicator mechanism we will link the changed on date attribute (defined as timestamp in IBP) with the Product Deletion Indicator attribute (defined as nvarchar in IBP).

In the Mapping Expression panel, chain the following functions:

  1. substring – Trim the timestamp to YYYY-MM-DD format (10 characters)
  2. compareDates – Compare the result against currentDate, specifying the expected date format for both inputs
  3. equals(number) – Check the output of compareDates:
    • 0 → dates are equal (record updated today)
    • -1 → changed-on date is earlier than today
    • 1 → changed-on date is later than today
  4. If – If the result is -1, set the Deletion Indicator to X; otherwise leave it blank

 

🔧 Combining the Transformed Payload with Write Parameters

After the Message Mapping, your message carries transformed IBP master data. Before calling the write iFlows, you need to merge this payload with the 'Write' configuration parameters (batch name, IBP destination, master data type, etc.) passed via a Content Modifier step.

To do this cleanly:

   Filter the Message

Add a Filter building block immediately after the Message Mapping step.

  • Set the XPath expression to: //item

This strips the outer XML hierarchy (header node, <IBPReadMasterDataPRODUCT> wrapper) and retains only the <item> nodes carrying the actual master data values.

sidenote : to see this payload info above you need to enable Trace logs and deploy your whole iflow. see here how to enable these detailed logs.

   Save the Payload as an Exchange Property

Add a Content Modifier step. Create an exchange property with:

Field

Value

Name

payload (or any name you prefer)

Source Type

Expression

Source Value

${body}

Data Type

java.lang.String

Exchange properties persist throughout the entire flow and can be referenced at any later stage — this is the key to combining the two messages.

 

📤 Step 3 – Write Data to IBP

With the transformed data saved as an exchange property, you're ready to write back to IBP. Call the following reusable iFlows in sequence, again via RequestReply + ProcessDirect, as highlighted in the Integration guide for SAP IBP reusable integration flows examples.

#

iFlow

Purpose

1

SAP_IBP_Write_-_Create_Batches

Creates a batch header (name, ID, operation type: insert/update, replace, or delete) with no data yet. The batch is already visible in IBP's Data Integration app at this point.

2

SAP_IBP_Write_-_Post_Data

Creates batch items and loads master data or key figure records to the IBP staging tables.

3

SAP_IBP_Write_-_Process_Posted_Data

Triggers IBP's post-processing step to move data from staging tables to application tables, making it visible in planning.

You need two Content Modifier steps to supply the required parameters:

Before Create_Batches: Provide the batch key, batch name, command, and IBP destination. This step is already mentioned above as it also references your saved exchange property to carry the master data payload forward.

Before Post_Data: Provide the batch ID, master data type/prefix, and selected attributes. To inject the previously saved payload into the message body, simply reference the exchange property directly: ${property.payload}

💡 Flexibility at the Write Step

Because the IBP destination is a configurable parameter in the Content Modifier, it's straightforward to:

  • Send data to a different IBP tenant (e.g., read from Production, write to Test)
  • Send data to a different version, using the PlanningAreaVersion parameter (for master data types that support version-specific MD)
  • Send data to a different master data type (e.g., from pre-prod Product MD to quality Product MD) by adjusting the MasterDataPrefix / MasterDataType parameters

 

🔒 Step 4 – Close the Read Session

Always end your flow by calling SAP_IBP_Read_-_Close. This releases the server-side read session opened by Initialize and frees up resources on the IBP backend.

 

Summary

Initialize → Fetch_Data → [Message Mapping + Filter + Save Property] → Create_Batches → Post_Data → Process_Posted_Data → Close

This pattern gives you a single, configurable integration flow that can cover a wide range of IBP-to-IBP scenarios — housecleaning, data copies, version fan-out, cross-tenant refreshes — simply by adjusting parameters in your Content Modifier steps and adapting the Message Mapping logic.

I hope this walkthrough gives you a solid foundation for building your own IBP-to-IBP flows with Cloud Integration. Feel free to share questions or your own variations in the comments below!

entry.title: New course: Positioning SAP B2B Self Service Portal
entry.content:
We’re excited to introduce a brand-new learning opportunity for sales and pre-sales professionals:

Positioning SAP B2B Self Service Portal 

Why SAP B2B Self Service Portal?

Business buyers expect consumer‑grade, 24/7 self‑service. The SAP B2B Self‑Service Portal helps companies meet those expectations while reducing the cost to serve and accelerating revenue. 

What Will You Learn?

This focused course goes beyond product features. After completing this course learners will be able to:

  • Understand the core capabilities and user experience of the SAP B2B Self‑Service Portal.
  • See how end‑to‑end order information is surfaced and how it improves transparency and response times.
  • Identify key business value drivers and articulate benefits such as reduced cost‑to‑serve, higher digital adoption, and revenue impact.
  • Leverage real‑world customer success stories to handle objections and inspire adoption.

Who Should Enroll?

  • Sales consultants working with SAP customers or prospects
  • Pre-sales consultants looking to stand out with specialized knowledge
  • Anyone eager to understand how SAP B2B Self Service Portal drives smarter business operations

Learn Today!

Ready to master SAP OMS positioning? Start learning now and take the first step toward equipping yourself with the knowledge and strategies you need to confidently position SAP B2B Self Service Portal.

 

We hope you enjoy this course and encourage you to share your valuable feedback with us. Thank you!

entry.title: Clarifications Needed on Universal Allocation(Manage allocation app)Capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Clou

  1. Is there an option to enable approval workflows for allocation cycles or allocation execution?
  2. Can we define or use a new Journal Entry Type for postings generated by Universal Allocation?
  3. Is it possible to allocate from one Statistical Project to another Statistical Project?
  4. Is there any way to manually enter a custom Document Header Text as an identifier during the allocation run?

Thanks

Ribin  KP

entry.title: IBP Integration with SAP Integration Suite – Your One-Stop Reference
entry.content:
👋 Welcome

This blog entry is a living index of resources covering SAP IBP integration using SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration). Whether you are just getting started or looking for guidance on a specific scenario, this is the place to find the right articles.

The goal of this entry is simple: as the series grows — with new posts from myself and from fellow community contributors — this page will be updated to reflect the latest content, so you always have a single reference point.

💡 Bookmark this page and check back regularly. It will be updated as new blog entries are published.

 

📚 Blog Series Index

 

🔷 Foundation & Setup

These entries cover the fundamentals: understanding why Cloud Integration is relevant for IBP and how to get your environment configured.

Blog Entry Description
Enhancing SAP IBP Integration – Evolution and InnovationBroader perspective on the evolution of the IBP integration landscape, covering both current capabilities and the direction of innovation. A good read for understanding the strategic context.
SAP IBP – New Integration Option: Introducing SAP Cloud Integration from BTPOverview of Cloud Integration as a new integration option for IBP. The starting point for the series — explains the shift in integration strategy and what CI brings to the table.

🔷 S/4HANA (private cloud or on-premise) to IBP Integration

Hands-on guidance for creating integration flows based on SAP delivered reusable content, focusing on S/4HANA private/on-prem as source or target.

Blog Entry Description
Configuring SAP Cloud Integration for an IBP-to-S/4HANA On-Premise (Private Cloud) scenarioStep-by-step configuration guide for connecting Cloud Integration to an S/4HANA on-premise or private cloud system from IBP. Covers connectivity setup, credentials, and key configuration parameters.
Creating Your First iFlow in Cloud Integration for an IBP / S/4HANA On-Prem scenarioA practical walkthrough for building your first integration flow between IBP and S/4HANA on-premise, using the reusable iFlow building blocks available in the IBP package.
IBP Integration Using SAP Cloud Integration – Fetch Product MD from Add-OnPractical guide to fetching Product master data from the S/4HANA integration add-on and loading it into IBP via Cloud Integration.

🔷 IBP-to-IBP Integration

Scenarios where IBP is both source and target — for data copies, transformations, housecleaning, and more.

Blog Entry Description
Modelling IBP-to-IBP Integration Flows with Cloud IntegrationHow to model a full IBP-to-IBP flow using the reusable iFlows: reading data with Initialize / Fetch / Close, transforming it via Message Mapping (including a housecleaning example), and writing it back using Create Batches / Post Data / Process Posted Data.

🔷 File-Based Integration

Covers integration scenarios involving file exchange via SFTP.

Blog Entry Description
IBP File-Based Integration with SFTP Server via Integration Suite – Cloud IntegrationHow to implement file-based integration for IBP using an SFTP server and Cloud Integration, including flow design, adapter configuration, and file handling patterns.

 

Want to Contribute?

If you have written or are planning a blog entry on IBP integration with Integration Suite, feel free to leave a comment below with your link and a short description. I will review it and add it to the index.

SAP Integrated Business Planning Cloud Integration SAP BTP ABAP environment SAP S/4HANA SAP Integration Suite

entry.title: nJIT S2P with External Replenishment (Component Material and IM Managed Warehouse)
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I am trying to set up nJIT Supply to Production Process with external replenishment, considering component material and an IM-Managed Warehouse with TM integration.

Standard process with all variants can be found in the following link: End-To-End (E2E) External Replenishment Process for JIT/JIS Call | SAP Help Portal

I was able to:

  1. Create Purchase Scheduling Agreement
  2. Maintain Delivery schedule and trigger Freight Unit creation on TM side
  3. Create JIT Call and trigger SOAP message to the supplier
  4. Create Consignment Order with reference to the JIT call and Freight Order on TM side and trigger Inbound Delivery creation on LE side

However, JIT Call internal status is not updated to "Inbound Delivery Created". It is still with status "JIT Call created".

Additional information: JIT control cycle creates with standard action control S2P_ACTRL & Logistics Integration Relevance "Component Materials" and purchase scheduling agreement assigned.

Any hints on what is missing here?

Thanks!

entry.title: Missing Shared Dimensions in XML Export from HANA Studio
entry.content:
SAP HANA studio (version  2.3.65)

Hi Everyone,

I’m trying to generate a complete XML dump of my SAP HANA views (Aanalytic view)  for documentation and migration purposes.

Here’s my scenario:

I have a Data Foundation with several shared dimensions coming through joins.These shared dimensions are also present in the Semantics layer and used in downstream views.However, when I export the objects to XML from HANA Studio, I cannot find some of these shared dimension fields in the XML dump.

It looks like the fields exist and are being used downstream, but they are missing from the exported XML.

Has anyone faced this issue before?
Is there a reliable way to include all shared dimensions (even those coming through joins and used in downstream views) in the XML export?

Any tips, workarounds, or settings I might be missing would be really helpful.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Time Account Type Validity in Successfactors
entry.content:
Hi LA_01
1. Create a Time Account first  - Provide External Name, workflow Configurations, Unit, Country, Permitted Fractions for Unit Day; 

2. Go ahead and create Take Rules, like backdate should allowed, negative should be allowed or not.

3. Time Account Type - Same External Name, Unit : Days, Account Creation Type: Recurring, Automatic Account Creation - No Automation, Accrual Creation offset -1, Hire Rule, Termination Rule, Accrual Rule (Ensure you config it properly).

4. While creating a  Leave Account : Account Validity & Bookable Period for 1st Year be from 1-Apr-xx to 31-Dec-xx; you can do a manual PEP on 31-Dec or 1-Jan

5. While you create a Next Year Leave Account : Account Validity & Bookable Period be 1-Jan-xx to 31-Dec-xx, Set PEP Automatic from this year onwards.

Regards
Ajay Kulkarni

entry.title: 🎙️ Série da comunidade: Somos SAP Community Curitiba
entry.content:
Comunidade não é só evento.
É troca, conexão e pessoas que constroem juntas.


Pensando nisso, iniciamos a série Somos SAP Community Curitiba, um espaço para dar voz às pessoas que ajudam a fortalecer o ecossistema SAP na nossa cidade.


Mais do que falar sobre tecnologia, queremos compartilhar histórias reais, trajetórias, desafios e conexões que fazem a comunidade acontecer.
💛Valorizar quem constrói a comunidade
🔗Fortalecer conexões entre profissionais SAP
🚀Inspirar novas pessoas a entrarem no ecossistema
🌱Mostrar que comunidade é colaboração, não competição
👩‍💻Dar visibilidade a talentos locais


📌Este é o post de referência da série.

Aqui você encontra o template para quem for convidado(a) a participar.

📌COMO PARTICIPAR DA SÉRIE
Se você for convidado(a), siga este formato no seu post:


1️⃣Título do post
Comece seu post com o título:
Somos SAP Community Curitiba – [Seu nome]


2️⃣Comece citando este post de referência
Inclua o link deste post no início do seu texto.
Exemplo:
Esta publicação faz parte da série Somos SAP Community Curitiba.
O template da série está neste post de referência: [link deste post]


3️⃣Mencione quem te convidou
Comece dizendo quem te convidou para a série e mencione o post da pessoa.
Exemplo:
Fui convidado(a) por [@nome da pessoa] para participar da série
Somos SAP Community Curitiba.
Você pode conferir o post dela/dele aqui: [link do post]


4️⃣Responda às 3 perguntas

  • Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?
  • O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?
  • Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?


5️⃣Continue a corrente
Convide no mínimo 1 pessoa da comunidade para continuar a série.
Se quiser, você pode convidar mais pessoas também.
Exemplo:
Passando a corrente 🚀
Agora eu convido [@nome da pessoa] a contar um pouco da sua história e da sua conexão com a SAP Community Curitiba.


Minha pergunta para você é:
[Pergunta livre]


6️⃣Opcional
Você também pode incluir no seu post:
📷Uma foto
🔗Suas redes sociais


💡Dicas

  • Ao convidar alguém, lembre-se de avisar a pessoa para que ela saiba que foi convidada a continuar a série.
  • Fique à vontade para compartilhar o link do seu post também no LinkedIn, usando as hashtags da série para ajudar a ampliar a visibilidade da comunidade.

 

Hashtags da série

#SAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunity


Se você faz parte da SAP Community Curitiba, essa corrente também é sua.

Vamos construir essa história juntos.

Comunidade se constrói com pessoas.
E agora queremos ouvir as histórias de quem faz a SAP Community Curitiba acontecer.
Se você faz parte da comunidade e gostaria de participar da série, deixe seu nome nos comentários deste post.
Será um prazer ampliar ainda mais essa corrente.

Assim você fica por dentro de eventos, encontros, conteúdos e iniciativas da SAP Community Curitiba.

entry.title: 9 ways to create Interface Fact Sheets in SAP LeanIX
entry.content:
Interface Fact Sheets in LeanIX can explain the data flow between two applications on a logical and on a technical level. A logical interface would be "Replicate BusinessPartner Create from SAP S/4HANA Finance to SalesForce". A technical interface might be one specific integration component (e.g. Integration Flow on SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration or the API Proxy in front of SalesForce on API Management) which can be only a part of the logical interface.

 

The criteria to differentiate the integration options is 

  • level of automation
  • granularity (logical and/or technical interface)
  • which data sources are supported

Here´s the list:

 

Option

Automation & Granularity

Supported Data Sources

1. Manual Entry 

manual (logical)

n/a – no integration used

2. Bulk Import

semi-automated (technical)

n/a – no integration used

3. GraphQL API

automated (logical/technical)

MAKE: whatever you program

4. LeanIX Adapter

automated (logical/technical)

MAKE: whatever you program

5. Standard Content: NTT Data

automated (logical only)

SAP IS: Integration Assessment

6. Standard Content: RealCoreautomated (technical only)SAP IS: Cloud Integration
SAP IS: API Management
SAP Process Orchestration
7. SAP Cloud ALMsemi-automated (technical only)Monitored SAP-Systems

8. LeanIX MCP Server

automated (logical/technical)

Create not possible yet, potentially various data sources in future

9. WHINT Integration Cockpit

automated (logical & technical)

Multiple: SAP and Non-SAP Integration Layer Components

You can find a more detailed explanation in the Integration Excellence blog: https://www.integration-excellence.com/how-to-feed-sap-leanix-with-interface-fact-sheets/

entry.title: Technical validation request: multiple copies of the same legacy system integrating simultaneously
entry.content:
Technical validation request: multiple copies of the same legacy system integrating simultaneously to S/4HANA (RISE) QAS, end-to-end SD flow (synchronous)

I’m designing an integration solution for a customer and would like your help validating technical feasibility — both the overall architecture and the specific integrations — before proceeding.

Scenario

  • Customer on SAP RISE with S/4HANA (QAS).
  • Up to 10 copies of the same legacy system (Instance 1 to Instance 10) integrating simultaneously against the same QAS environment.
  • Full Sales flow, synchronous: ATP Check → Sales Order → Delivery → PGI → Billing → Status Return.

Proposed architecture (summary)

  • Central hub on SAP BTP using SAP Integration Suite (iFlows per process), identifying each legacy copy via headers:
    • X-Sender-System (business logical system)
    • X-Sender-Instance (copy index: 1..10)
  • SAP API Management for authentication, rate limiting, and distinct credentials per instance.
  • On S/4HANA QAS:
    • Communication System and Communication Arrangement per instance (up to 10 in parallel).
    • Use standard S/4 OData APIs for the flow: API_ATP_CHECK, API_SALES_ORDER_SRV, API_OUTBOUND_DELIVERY_SRV, API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV.
    • SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) for functional monitoring and traceability, using CorrelationID + SenderInstance as key fields (to isolate reprocessing per instance).

Note: The proposal is fully synchronous end-to-end for testing; no messaging/events here.

Example flow (synchronous, high level)

  1. Legacy Instance n calls ATP; the hub validates headers/credentials, forwards to S/4, and returns the response.
  2. Sales Order creation via API_SALES_ORDER_SRV; confirmation returns synchronously to the same instance.
  3. Delivery creation and PGI via logistics APIs; confirmations return to the caller.
  4. Billing via API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV; document number is returned to the corresponding instance.
  5. The entire cycle is traceable in AIF and Integration Suite monitoring by instance (SenderInstance) and CorrelationID.

Questions for the community

  1. Feasibility on RISE (Cloud Private Edition):
    • Are there any platform, tenant, or contractual constraints in RISE that would prevent this hub pattern with multiple instances of the same legacy calling QAS in parallel?
  2. Coverage of S/4HANA OData APIs (RISE):
    • Do the listed OData APIs (ATP, Sales Order, Outbound Delivery, Billing) support the full, 100% synchronous flow in this context?
    • Are there process steps that typically require alternatives (e.g., BAPI/IDoc) even in synchronous scenarios?
  3. Scale of Communication Systems/Arrangements:
    • Is it feasible to have up to 10 Communication Systems/Arrangements active for the same SD scenario in QAS simultaneously, one per legacy instance, without practical limitations?
  4. Isolation in AIF:
    • Does AIF support isolating reprocessing per instance (via key fields SenderInstance + CorrelationID), ensuring reprocessing for one instance does not affect others running in parallel?
  5. iFlow parameterization:
    • Do iFlows with Externalized Parameters per instance (endpoints, credentials, toggles) work on the RISE BTP tenant as designed? Any best practices to separate 10 instances without “exploding” the number of artifacts?

Any additional guidance is welcome

  • Naming best practices (Communication System/Arrangement per instance).
  • Practical throughput/concurrency limits in QAS for parallel SD testing.
  • Lessons learned for 100% synchronous SD flows (especially for Delivery/PGI/Billing via OData).

entry.title: Installing and extending an ABAP MCP Server by vibe-coding with Claude Code and Google Antigravity
entry.content:
On one rainy morning I mentioned to my Google Antigravity Agent (Claude Opus 4.6 Thinking):

"I hear people are experimenting with connecting to SAP Systems via MCP. They use ABAP Development Tools API as a way in. Could you help me set up something like that for my ABAP Trial system?"

It searched the Web and found Mario Andreschak's MCP-ABAP-ADT solution on GitHub: https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-adt. Then it came up with the following implementation plan and asked a few questions:

I approved the plan and answered the questions. After a while, it was done cloning Mario's Repository and installing:

I updated the Password and Client in the *.env file and switched to Claude Code, who (Don't anthropomorphise computers, they hate it!) can act as an MCP Client. Claude Code was able to connect to my ABAP Trial using Mario's MCP Server.

Not having studied Mario's documentation, I didn't realise his MCP Server was for reading only. I asked Claude Code to add a comment line to an ABAP Program, just as a test. It said it couldn't do it, on account of there being no WRITE, and offered to extend the MCP Server with WRITE capability. I said yes.

It didn't take long for Claude to do this, but I hit my rate limit and had to wait until the afternoon (Claude Code sessions are 5-hour windows with a limit on tokens. In addition, there are weekly limits).

There was a bit of a kerfuffle regarding the locking, but Claude Code managed to sort it all out by himself.

And sure enough, the test ABAP Program was updated with a comment. Actually, two comments - one was there from before, when Claude was testing the API via CURL.

As I was again nearing my Claude Code usage limits, I switched back to Google Antigravity. I asked the agent there to add even more WRITE tools: For Classes and Interfaces, plus a tool to create Programs from scratch. My request was dispatched rather swiftly.

I asked the Agent what other functionalities could he add to the MCP Server. As one would expect, he listed some of the many that are supported by the ADT API:

All we have to do is ask 😊

 

P.S.:

- The SAP System was ABAP Cloud Developer Trial 2023,

- I used a Mac,

- Claude Code and Google Gemini paid plans. Not any fancy "Max" plans, just the regular 20-EUR-per-month ones.

entry.title: Re: Technical validation request: multiple copies of the same legacy system integrating simultaneous
entry.content:
General responses/questions -

  1. Is the caller prepared to handle errors/warnings returned by the SAP system?
  2. Why do the calls need to be bound through the entire SD end-to-end to a single instance if they are copies?
  3. IFlows cannot be deployed more than once because the IDs need to be unique, which means you would require 10 copies of each IFlow that is designed - that sounds like a maintenance nightmare IMO.
  4. I'd suggest reading through this three part blog series (part 1 link provided) by @MateuszPiotrowski - https://community.sap.com/t5/integration-blog-posts/jms-mastery-series-part-1-the-async-awakening/ba-p/14319670?trk=public_post_comment-text

 

Regards,

Ryan Crosby

entry.title: Eliminating Integration Debt with SAP Integration Suite: Strategy for an AI-Ready Enterprise
entry.content:
The Enterprise Integration Imperative

Enterprises are investing aggressively in AI — yet many discover a hidden constraint: their systems cannot move data fast enough, securely enough, or reliably enough to support it. The limiting factor is not AI capability. It is integration maturity.

Every enterprise today runs a complex, heterogeneous application landscape — cloud and on-premise, modern and legacy, purpose-built and inherited. Connecting these systems reliably, securely, and at scale is no longer a background IT concern. It is a strategic imperative — one that determines how fast an enterprise can respond to change, how confidently it can adopt AI, and how well it can govern the data that flows across its entire operation.

SAP Integration Suite was built for precisely this reality. Not as a tool to connect SAP systems to the world, but as a comprehensive, open, enterprise-grade integration platform capable of serving every connectivity need across your landscape — regardless of vendor, protocol, or deployment model. This article makes that case: what integration debt costs, what genuine breadth of connectivity looks like, and what security, governance, and resilience mean when they are built into the foundation rather than bolted on after the fact.

Defining the problem

Integration debt is a dimension of an organization's broader technical debt — measuring the distance between how its applications, processes, and data interact today and how they should: reflected not just in technical complexity, but in operational risk, security exposure, and strategic drag accumulated over time. It does not stem from a single cause. It is the cumulative result of multiple independent forces — each reasonable in isolation, collectively creating a landscape that is fragmented, ungoverned, and increasingly difficult to manage. Some of the typical ones are as follows:

1) Shadow Automation: Scripts, macros, RPA-based automation that are individual/team/BU managed, undocumented, ungoverned, un-auditable, prone to upgrade instability.

2) Legacy processes: Manual file uploads/extracts via ungoverned interfaces, Direct Database level updates/extracts, Email based data exchange. Each was built to solve a specific problem. Collectively they represent a governance and security gap.

3) Vendor supplied platform or tool, but built for a different era: Legacy VAN based EDI connections. The continued use of such platforms are not aligned with the real-time, API-driven ecosystems that modern partner and supplier relationships demand.

4) Low code/no code tools not purpose-built to serve as an integration layer: platforms designed for departmental productivity, repurposed as connective layer between enterprise systems. They are accessible, fast to deploy, but not designed for the security, governance for enterprise-grade integration needs.

5) Integration platform fragmentation:  When you solve the point-to-point problem by adding more integration tools, resulting in creation of platform-to-platform silos.

This fifth dimension — integration platform fragmentation — deserves a closer examination.

For much of the last two decades, the market and industry narrative positioned SAP's integration platform as a tool for connecting SAP systems to the outside world. The legacy of SAP PI/PO — associated with IDoc and RFC/BAPI mediation for ECC and S/4HANA — created an impression that if your integration scenario did not involve an SAP application at one end, you needed a different platform. That perception was historically understandable. It is now definitively outdated. The historical perception, however, had consequences. It drove organizations toward platform fragmentation — not always through shadow IT decisions, but through considered choices as well.

Some fragmentation followed specific classes of use-cases based platform choice: An API gateway or a file transfer solution, catering to specific business processes, or a message broker managed by plant operations requiring higher autonomy.

Some fragmentation followed the relentless pace of SaaS adoption due to the accelerated digital transformation in the last decade. Each new best-in-class platform adopted for a specific function required connections to existing systems. Each connection, built in isolation, added another node to a landscape that was becoming increasingly difficult to see, manage, and secure as a whole. Had a single, open, enterprise-grade integration platform been the established foundation, much of this complexity would have been absorbed rather than accumulated.

Some fragmentation followed acquisition — an EDI platform inherited with a business unit.

In some cases, a platform was adopted by individual departments moving faster than central IT could govern.

Each was a reasonable decision in its moment. However, the cost of operating multiple integration platforms runs far deeper than a licensing budget spread across vendors. It is the absence of a unified security model — authentication policies, certificate management configured separately on every platform, audited separately. It is the absence of unified operational monitoring — when a business process fails end-to-end, IT sees disconnected system alerts across multiple dashboards while the business sees a failed outcome. It is the talent fragility of institutional knowledge distributed across multiple platform specialisms, where every departure takes irreplaceable context with it. It is the organizational friction of negotiating every new integration requirement across multiple platform owners and governance processes — slowing the enterprise precisely when speed matters most.

And above all of this sits a question every enterprise leadership team is now asking: "are we AI-ready?" The answer depends less on which AI tools or models you have licensed and more on whether your data flows reliably, in real time, across your entire application landscape. A fragmented integration platform cannot provide that foundation. It can only provide fragmented data, at fragmented speed, with fragmented accountability. Integration debt is not just an IT cost. It is the invisible barrier between your enterprise and its AI ambitions.

One comprehensive, versatile, enterprise-grade integration platform — governed, secure, and capable enough to handle every connectivity need in your enterprise is the foundation of a mature digital architecture.


The Breadth: Every System, Every Protocol, Every Pattern

The problem section described an integration landscape shaped by accumulation of ungoverned tools, fragmented platforms, and decisions made without a unified foundation. The answer is not a better point-to-point tool. It is a platform with the breadth to make fragmentation unnecessary in the first place.

SAP Integration Suite is that platform — open, enterprise-grade, and designed to connect every application, process, and data in your landscape, regardless of vendor, protocol, or deployment model. Its breadth is best understood not as a list of adapters, but as coverage across every integration pattern and every layer of the modern enterprise architecture.

1. API-Led Connectivity — The Language of the Modern Enterprise

REST, OData, SOAP, HTTP — Integration Suite speaks the full API vocabulary of the modern enterprise. Whether you are connecting cloud-native SaaS platforms, exposing on-prem resources as managed APIs, or consuming partner and ecosystem APIs, Integration Suite handles every synchronous and asynchronous API interaction pattern out-of-the-box.

2. Event-Driven Architecture — Real-Time, at Enterprise Scale

The emergence of event-driven architecture is one of the defining paradigms in enterprise integration technology today. Business processes no longer wait for scheduled batch runs or manual triggers — they respond to events as they happen, across systems, in real time.

SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh is SAP's flagship offering to support the event driven journey of enterprises. It is an enterprise-grade event streaming and management platform that enables real-time, event-driven communication across distributed systems and applications. It complements traditional integration patterns like API-led integration. The platform's advanced features, such as dynamic message routing, message replay enable organizations to achieve seamless, real-time business processes.

Beyond Advanced Event Mesh, Integration Suite enables connectivity to the full ecosystem of enterprise messaging infrastructure: Apache Kafka, IBM MQ, RabbitMQ, Apache ActiveMQ, Azure Service Bus, Google Pub/Sub, Amazon Kinesis, and Amazon EventBridge — via purpose-built native adapters. Whatever messaging infrastructure your enterprise already runs, Integration Suite connects to it seamlessly.

3. File, Batch, and Legacy Exchange — The Unglamorous Workhorses That Still Run Your Business

Not every integration is real-time. Not every system speaks the API language. A significant portion of enterprise data exchange still moves through file-based and batch processes — and will for the foreseeable future.

Integration Suite supports SFTP, SMB, and mail-based exchange protocols natively, alongside AS2 and AS4 for secure, standards-compliant B2B document exchange. AS2 and AS4 are the modern, encrypted, auditable replacement for the legacy VAN-based EDI connections — giving you a governed, standards-based path to modernize B2B connectivity without disrupting partner relationships.

4. Database Connectivity — Reaching the Data Where It Lives

Not every system exposes a clean API. At times, platforms require direct database-level connectivity to participate in business process integration. Integration Suite connects natively to a range of databases via DB specific adapters: Oracle (cloud and on-premise), Microsoft SQL Server (cloud and on-premise), PostgreSQL, IBM DB2, SAP HANA (cloud and on-premise), MongoDB, Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon DynamoDB, and Azure CosmosDB.

5. SaaS Connectivity — application specific connectors

At first glance, connecting to a SaaS application seems simple: make an HTTP call to its REST API. In reality, that call is only the beginning. Each application has its own authentication mechanisms, pagination rules, rate limits, error formats, and evolving data models. Every new SaaS platform requires developers to learn those nuances, build custom logic around them, and maintain that logic as APIs change over time. What appears simple at the surface quickly becomes a growing layer of hidden complexity.

SAP Integration Suite removes that burden through purpose-built SaaS adapters. These are not generic HTTP connectors. They are pre-configured and pre-tested adapters that understand the specific behaviours of each platform — handling authentication, data structures, and platform-specific constraints out of the box.

The result is not just faster implementation. It is reduced long-term maintenance, lower risk during API changes, and a standardized integration approach across your SaaS landscape.

The coverage spans the full breadth of the enterprise SaaS landscape: Salesforce and Salesforce Pub/Sub, ServiceNow, Workday, BambooHR, NetSuite, Coupa, Anaplan, HubSpot, Jira, Zendesk, Docusign, Shopify, BigCommerce, SugarCRM, Splunk, Snowflake, Box, Dropbox, Microsoft Dynamics CRM, Microsoft SharePoint, Microsoft OneDrive, Microsoft Teams, Microsoft Outlook, Microsoft OneNote, Slack, MongoDB, RabbitMQ, and many more.

In practice, this means that when a new SaaS platform enters your enterprise, you are not starting from scratch. You are extending a governed foundation — not adding another layer of custom code.

6. Hyperscaler Connectivity — Your bridge to IaaS/PaaS platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP, ...)

Most enterprises today do not operate on a single cloud platform. Their workloads are distributed across multiple providers — shaped by architectural choices, financial considerations, and historical decisions accumulated over time. Each hyperscaler has its own native services, its own messaging infrastructure, its own storage and database layer.

Integration Suite connects natively to all the major platforms: AWS via S3, SQS, SNS, SWF, Kinesis, EventBridge, and DynamoDB adapters; Azure via Service Bus, Cosmos DB, and Storage adapters; Google Cloud via BigQuery, Cloud Storage, and Pub/Sub adapters.

This is not generic HTTP connectivity to cloud endpoints. These are hyperscaler-native adapters that speak in the language of native protocols, authentication models, and service behaviours of each cloud platform. Integration Suite can serve as a governed, unified layer across your multi-cloud infrastructure — the single place where cross-cloud data flows are defined, secured, monitored, and managed.

In effect, SAP Integration Suite is your cross-cloud integration fabric.

7. AI Connectivity — The Integration Backbone for the Intelligent Enterprise

As enterprises move from AI experimentation to AI operationalization, AI itself becomes a powerful capability to be integrated into core business processes that span multiple applications. Integration Suite's dedicated AI Adapter connects natively to SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub and to third-party AI providers — making it the governed integration layer through which AI enters your business processes.

8. Open Connectors — 170+ Additional Applications, One Normalized Layer

For applications not covered by purpose-built adapters, Open Connectors provides normalized, virtualized connectivity to over 170 additional non-SAP cloud applications through a unified adapter layer — with consistent authentication handling, normalized data models, and a single development experience regardless of the underlying application.

9. Pre-Built Integration Content — Accelerating Time to Value

Connectivity is necessary but not sufficient. What enterprises also need is the ability to move fast — to implement integrations without starting from a blank canvas every time. Integration Suite's pre-built integration content — available through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub — provides a library of ready-to-use integration flows, APIs , events, and much more covering both SAP and non-SAP scenarios. Rather than building integration logic from scratch, teams start from a SAP-delivered baseline and configure rather than code. This is what out-of-the-box integration capability means in practice: faster implementation, lower technical debt, and standardized patterns that are easier to maintain, upgrade, and govern over time.

What This Breadth Means in Practice

Virtually no system in your enterprise landscape — legacy or modern, on-premise or private cloud or public cloud, SAP or non-SAP — is outside the reach of Integration Suite. When a business unit adopts a new platform, there is almost certainly a supported, governed path to integrate it.

SAP Integration Suite is the best positioned platform on which the integration debt problem — in all five forms described earlier — finds its answer.


The Depth: Security, Governance, Resilience

Connectivity establishes reach. Security, governance, and resilience establish trust. Here is how Integration Suite earns it.

1. Security

Integration Suite secures the full perimeter of your integration landscape — from user access and credentials, to securing the message payloads, to backend connectivity, to your externally exposed APIs.

  • Access & Identity Control: Integration Suite governs access at multiple levels — not just who can log in, but also what they can do. And at the identity layer, SAP Cloud Identity Services provides centralized identity and access management across SAP BTP — supporting Single Sign-On and identity federation across your enterprise landscape. Role-collection based user management controls who can design, deploy, monitor, and operate integrations across each environment. Access policies feature goes further — defining which specific integration packages and artifacts each team member can access, enabling precise control within large, distributed integration teams. Together this follows the model of Principle of Least Privilege.
  • Credential & Key Management: Integration Suite eliminates the need for placing hardcoded credentials in integration scenarios — one of the most silent and costly vulnerabilities in custom-built integrations — through a range of supported artifacts such as Security material, Keystore and Secure parameter.
  • Message-Level security: Encryption, decryption, digital signing, and verification are available out-of-the-box — ensuring data is protected and verifiable at the payload level, not just at the transport layer.
  • Secure backend connectivity: Cloud Connector helps extend secure, governed connectivity to on-premise systems without opening firewall ports. It supports  fine grain access control of what backend resources to expose via what protocol. Cloud Connector supports Principal Propagation to propagate your end-user context to the backend to ensure role based resource access and auditability.
  • API Security: API Management capability of Integration Suite protects enterprise API assets from OWASP threats through measures such as rate limiting, quota enforcement, and IP allowlisting — with full visibility into API consumption through an enterprise developer portal and API analytics. The recently released AI-based features such as Anomaly detection and API traffic prediction, helps API product owners derive actionable insights.

2. Governance

Integration Suite covers the full governance lifecycle — assessing your integration landscape, standardizing how integrations are designed, controlling how changes move across environments, monitoring operations, and maintaining the audit record. A governance framework is only as effective as the organizational commitment behind it. Integration Suite provides the tools — the discipline to apply them consistently is what transforms a platform investment into a governed integration landscape.

  • Integration Assessment (ISA-M): SAP's Integration Solution Advisory Methodology, now part of Integration Assessment capability provides a structured framework for designing, assessing and enforcing your integration strategy, to take your organization's integration maturity to a higher level. This includes defining the reference domains, styles and use-case patterns — before a single new integration is built. Governance starts here.
  • Design Guidelines & BTP Guidance Framework: Guidelines to Design Enterprise-Grade Integration Flows and BTP Guidance Framework ensure integrations are built to a consistent standard from day one.
  • Transport Management using CTMS: SAP Cloud Transport Management Service (CTMS) is a cloud-native BTP solution to help in robust change management.
  • Operational Visibility with Cloud ALM: SAP Cloud ALM provides the operational control tower for your integration landscape — integration flow monitoring, technical health monitoring (such as JMS resources, certificate expiry), and business process monitoring that correlates integration activity to the business outcomes it supports.
  • Compliance & Audit — SAP Audit Log Service: SAP Audit Log Service maintains an audit record of every significant system change event happening in your tenant.

3. Resilience

  • Integration Suite is built on a proven open-source foundation, with the runtime reliability, message delivery guarantees, and deployment flexibility that business-critical integration demands.
  • The Apache Camel stack: SAP Integration Suite is built on Apache Camel — a proven open-source integration framework.
  • Quality of Service: Integration Suite supports a range of quality of service options, as required by your integration scenarios - be it best effort, guaranteed delivery, and so on.
  • Retry Handling: Standard retry handling for asynchronous scenarios, built into the Pipeline concept, giving you a capability familiar to you if also operate an SAP PI/PO landscape.
  • Structured Error Handling using SAP AIF: SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) provides a structured, governed error handling for your ERP processes. A powerful feature of AIF is that it empowers business users to monitor interfaces and troubleshoot issues without IT, thereby reducing the time needed for error handling.
  • Infrastructure Resilience with In-metro DR: High availability is ensured through multi-availability zone deployment within a metro region — with defined RTO and RPO commitments, making Integration Suite the viable backbone for your business-critical processes.
  • Deployment Flexibility using Edge Integration Cell: For enterprises with data residency requirements, or processes that must execute on-premise, the Edge Integration Cell provides a on-premise or private cloud deployment option, in the hyperscaler platform of your choice.


The AI-Ready Enterprise Starts Here

Every meaningful AI initiative your organization is pursuing — whether Generative AI based or Agentic AI — shares one non-negotiable prerequisite: connected, governed, real-time data flowing reliably across your entire enterprise landscape.

This is not a future requirement. It is a present one. Enterprises that are moving fastest on AI are not doing so because they have the most advanced models. They are doing so because they resolved their integration foundation first — establishing the governed, real-time data flows that AI depends upon before AI needed them.

SAP Integration Suite is the best positioned platform in your landscape capable of connecting every system, governing every flow, and providing the end-to-end data reliability that AI operationalization demands — regardless of whether those systems are SAP or not.

The enterprises that treat integration modernization and AI readiness as the same investment — made once, on the right platform — will arrive at AI-at-scale ahead of those that treat them as separate workstreams and rebuild the foundation twice. The integration foundation you build today is the AI infrastructure you will depend on tomorrow.

entry.title: Latest People Profile in the H1 Release: Concerns and Request for Delay until H2 Release
entry.content:
As we prepare for the H1 Release of the Latest People Profile, our team has identified several gaps that may create challenges for HR teams and employees. We appreciate SAP’s commitment to modernizing the profile, but the 2026 H1 version introduces limitations that reduce usability and roll back progress many customers—ours included—have made.

Key Issues

9-Box Grid Not Supported
The removal of the 9-box grid takes away an essential tool for talent reviews, calibration, and succession planning. This is core functionality our teams rely on for quick, informed decision-making.

Limited Card Customization
Cards now display only answers, with labels hidden behind “View more.” This makes information harder to understand and increases the risk of misinterpretation.

Unlabeled Dates
Dates appear without showing whether they are start, end, or last modified, which creates confusion and potential errors.

These changes reduce the clarity and flexibility that the existing People Profile provides.

Fixes Coming — But Only in H2

SAP has indicated that many of these issues will be addressed in the H2 Release in November. While it’s good news that fixes are planned, it leaves customers navigating six months of reduced functionality, followed by another transition when updates arrive. This adds unnecessary change management and stakeholder frustration—especially for teams that recently invested in improving their People Profile.

Recommendation

Customers should have the option to delay adopting the Latest People Profile until H2, when the missing functionality will be restored. This flexibility would:

  • Reduce disruption for HR teams
  • Avoid confusion for employees
  • Allow customers to adopt the profile when it’s fully ready
  • Strengthen confidence in the release

We support SAP’s overall direction, but a mandatory rollout with known limitations places avoidable burdens on customers. Allowing organizations to opt in at the right time would turn this into a much smoother, more thoughtful transition.

entry.title: Re: JMS Mastery Series Part 2 - The JMS Revolution
entry.content:
I've been party to a scenario where the EOIO trap would periodically block time-critical integration as soon as one message failed (voice picking).  I warned against using EOIO (not required for the scenario) for that sole reason, but it was done to avoid a dependency on the BASIS team when the generic XI queues would get stuck in the back-end because they were generally slow to respond or were unexperienced, so it was implemented with a custom queue in PO which would operate in an EOIO fashion.  Every few months they would have a several hour pause before people noticed because of a failure in EOIO.  At some point after I left, former colleagues shared that they had quietly heeded my initial advice, eventually.

Wanted to share my example and acknowledge the second blog part is great as well, and you shared quality in-depth information including missteps to avoid.

entry.title: Re: Hana SQL Command Network Protocol Deprecation/Sunset
entry.content:
Evolution, Not Extinction: The State of HANA Connectivity in 2026

Hi Kevin,

Welcome to the SAP Community! This is a very insightful question. As we move through 2026, the confusion around protocol "sunsets" often stems from how SAP is shifting its connectivity standards to support modern, stable landscapes.

To give you a definitive answer: No, the SQL Command Network Protocol is not being "shut down." It remains the core engine of HANA communication. However, the way we are encouraged to consume it is evolving to ensure better system stability.

Here are three key points to clarify your concerns:

1. The Python Driver is NOT abandoned The confusion regarding GitHub usually refers to older open-source wrappers. The official SAP-maintained driver is hdbcli. It is very much active (currently at version 2.27+) and is the standard for high-performance Python connectivity to both HANA Cloud and On-Premise. You can find it on PyPI via pip install hdbcli.

2. From "Direct Access" to "Stable Operations" The "sunset" feeling likely comes from SAP's push toward a Service-Layer approach (like OData or the Cloud Application Programming Model). In my experience leading AMS delivery, moving away from "thick" direct SQL connections toward these managed service layers is essential for predictive operations. It reduces the "incident-driven" firefighting that often happens when custom scripts break after a HANA upgrade.

3. Strategic Alignment I have been documenting how these technical shifts impact the long-term stability of S/4HANA landscapes. If you are looking at how to align your technical skills with where SAP operations are heading, you might find these perspectives on "Future-Proofing" helpful:

  • Future-Proofing SAP AMS: A Strategic Framework: This covers why we must move from reactive support to predictive operations in S/4HANA environments.

  • Running SAP AMS Like a Product, Not a Support Factory: This explores the shift from "closing tickets" to ensuring process-level stability, something that becomes much easier when using official, supported connectivity methods like hdbcli.

Summary: The protocol is not going anywhere, but the Access Method is. Stick with the official hdbcli for Python, but keep an eye on service-oriented architecture to ensure your environment remains stable and "Future-Proof."

Hope this helps you navigate the roadmap!

entry.title: Re: How Buyers Discover Suppliers on SAP Business Network
entry.content:
Hi @Francois-Xavier ,

Thank you for your thoughtful comment and for sharing your perspective.

To address your question about usage: SAP Business Network Discovery is actively used by many suppliers on the network to respond to buyer postings and RFIs. In practice, many suppliers engage with Discovery by responding to matched buyer postings (RFIs) and direct RFIs generated from buyer searches, rather than using it purely as a standalone marketplace search experience. Because much of this activity happens through these targeted matches, its usage can be less visible from the outside, even though it is a common way suppliers connect with new buyers on the network.

While I’m not able to share detailed platform usage statistics externally at this time, we have seen strong results from customers using Discovery. For example, in the Graybar customer story, the supplier shared that:

“As of this year, we supported business growth using SAP Business Network Discovery, submitting 68 bids and winning 66 of those proposals from new customers in this community.”

This highlights how suppliers can successfully use Discovery to expand their customer base through the network.

Your point about search intent is highly valuable. Today, SAP Business Network, including Discovery, uses the UNSPSC classification system (version 25) to structure categories and match buyers with relevant suppliers. Suppliers are ranked primarily based on how closely their capabilities align with the commodity, class, and family levels of the buyer’s requirement, along with other factors such as profile relevance and location.

That said, we absolutely recognize that search experience and discoverability are critical for both buyers and suppliers. Feedback like yours is important, and the product team continuously works to refine how suppliers can be found, whether through taxonomy, improved search capabilities, or other discovery mechanisms.

Thank you again for engaging in the discussion and sharing your perspective.

Kind regards,
Amanda

entry.title: Re: JMS Mastery Series Part 3 - JMS in Production
entry.content:
@HaraldBoeing we do use the XI adapter for two interfaces here, but that is relatively sparing considering the few dozen integration points that are in place, and the only real reason is to make use of the attachment handling that is supported.  Two file based interfaces where the file part becomes decoupled by making use of the attachment handling, but still guaranteeing delivery because XI makes use of the JMS adapter.

Regarding EOIO, I have yet to encounter a single legitimate instance in my 18+ years of working in the integration space within SAP of an interface truly needing that functionality - seems to me more an artifact of very outdated practices that have never truly matured or been modernized.

entry.title: Re: Latest People Profile in the H1 Release: Concerns and Request for Delay until H2 Release
entry.content: Hi, I completely agree. The more I test, the more defects and issues I continue to uncover. I’m dreading having to inform HR about the removal of the 9 box and the inability for others (with proper permissions) to view Learning History on an employee’s profile. This functionality was taken away years ago, then restored after multiple enhancement requests - and now we are losing it again. I’m also finding new issues related to RBP. When the profile category is granted, several sections display “no data,” which is not how this functions today in my Prod instance (KB3722226 & KB3724571). Pay Targets remain visible even after removing visibility in RBP (KB3710879), with a fix not planned until 1H 2026. I’m attempting to clean up RBP while I’m already making extensive updates, but it’s difficult when the system isn’t functioning correctly. My bigger concern is how I’m expected to update all of our system documentation and training materials when the test site isn’t yet working well enough to use for creating documentation, videos, or other training materials. Ideally, I would roll everything out at once, including the latest version of editing and history - but there are still too many issues to enable it. It is simply not fully functional. Initially, I was more optimistic, but the more I test and review, the more discouraged I become. In my honest opinion, this is not ready for a universal rollout. I am already navigating significant change management challenges with the new Onboarding version and its ongoing issues. I cannot take on additional backlash from managers, employees, and HR over yet another forced rollout outside of my control. Please give customers more time - at least until these issues are fully resolved.

entry.title: Access CDS View data through SAP Integration Suite for Power BI / Excel without embedding credential
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am trying to expose data from an SAP CDS View through SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) so that it can be consumed from Power BI and Excel. It is compulsory to use Integration Suite.

The main constraint of the scenario is authentication.

The Power BI and Excel files will be shared with multiple users, so I cannot embed credentials (for example in VBA scripts or connection strings).
Instead, the idea is to expose the CDS data through Integration Suite as a controlled endpoint.

The desired architecture is:

 
Power BI / Excel
↓
SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration)
↓
SAP Gateway OData Service (generated from CDS View)
 

The CDS View is already published as an OData service in SAP Gateway, and it works correctly when accessed directly.

What I have tried so far

I tried two different approaches in Integration Suite.

1. OData → OData Integration Flow

First I created an integration flow using OData adapters on both sides.

Configuration:

Sender adapter

OData V2
Operation: Query (GET)

Receiver adapter

 

OData V2
Resource path: <EntitySet>
Query options: ${header.CamelHttpQuery}
Authentication: Basic (credential stored in Integration Suite)

Goal: create a transparent OData proxy.

Result:

  • The iFlow deployed successfully.

  • I was able to retrieve the metadata from the CDS view through this approach.

  • PowerBI detected the Set BUT gave Internal Server Error (Error no500), so I could not access the info (same problem if tried in browser).

2. HTTPS Sender → HTTP Receiver proxy

I then attempted a (supposely) simpler HTTP proxy approach.

Sender:

HTTPS
Address:
/powerbi/cds02/*
 
Integration flow:
HTTPS Sender
↓
Start
↓
Content Modifier
↓
HTTP Receiver

Content Modifier headers:

CamelHttpPath = ${header.CamelHttpPath}
CamelHttpQuery = ${header.CamelHttpQuery}
 

The idea was to dynamically forward the request path and query string.
In SAP Cloud Integration, the CamelHttpQuery header contains the query parameters from the original request URL.

Receiver configuration:

Address: https://<backend>/sap/opu/odata/sap/<SERVICE_NAME>
Authentication: Basic

Result:

  • The iFlow deploys successfully.

  • However every request returns: An internal server error occured: The MPL ID for the failed message is : (number)

So in practice this HTTP proxy approach does not reach the CDS data at all.

Current status

Summary of what I achieved so far:

Approach Result
OData → ODataMetadata reached (column names got), but not data in it (internal server error)
HTTPS → HTTP proxyRequests fail with internal server error

Questions

  1. What is the recommended architecture to expose CDS data through Integration Suite for consumption by Power BI / Excel?

  2. Is using Integration Suite as an OData proxy the correct approach in this scenario?

  3. Are there special considerations for handling $metadata and query parameters when forwarding OData requests through CPI?

  4. Is there a better approach to solve the authentication constraint (no embedded credentials in Excel / Power BI files)?

Any guidance or examples of similar implementations would be greatly appreciated. I could not even find any tutorials or anything (involving Integration Suite), though I guess it is something used in many companies (it is my first time doing this).

Thanks in advance!
