entry.title: Why Attend The Fabric of Data & AI: Insights from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone & More
entry.content:
JOIN US AT THE FABRIC OF DATA & AI ON MARCH 24, 2026, 11AM EDT | 11AM CET | 1PM SGT

AI without context is like walking to the car wash.  

You may have seen the viral video: someone asks ChatGPT whether to walk or drive to the car wash. AI analyzes distance, time, and efficiency, then recommends walking. It misses the obvious: you need the car there to wash it. 

It's funny, until you realize the same disconnect is happening in business decisions every day. AI can analyze data, optimize metrics, and execute quickly, but without understanding business context, it can provide technically correct answers that completely miss the mark. 

As organizations deploy AI agents, intelligent applications, and large language models, many are experiencing a critical gap: the data strategies that powered the last decade of innovation weren't designed for autonomous decision-making. 

The Fabric of Data & AI, a virtual summit on March 24, 2026, brings together industry leaders at the forefront of this transformation. Join Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer of SAP Data & Analytics, in conversation with leaders from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone, Team Liquid, Mead Johnson Nutrition, and the Open Data Institute. 

Here are three reasons you should attend. 

1. Understand why yesterday’s data strategies fall short 

For decades, data strategies have focused on extracting data into centralized systems for applications and dashboards. This approach served its purpose for traditional analytics, but it created a problem: the loss of important business context. 

When AI makes critical decisions, such as allocating inventory, prioritizing customers, and managing cash flow, it needs more than just accurate data. It needs to grasp the business relationships, priorities, and trade-offs that determine the right course of action. 

At this summit, you'll discover: 

  • What happens when business context gets lost as data moves across systems 
  • Why AI agents can optimize individually but create problems collectively 
  • How preserving business meaning by design creates a competitive advantage 

2. Learn from leaders solving the same challenges you face

You’ll hear directly from leaders who are building data and AI strategies in their organizations, including: 

  • Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer, SAP Data & Analytics 
  • Jannie Affeld, VP Finance Systems and ERP, Google 
  • Malin Persson, CIO & Head of Enterprise IT, Ericsson 
  • Ricard Rovira Marti, Head of Corporate IT Platforms, Vodafone 
  • Steve Arhancet, Co-CEO and Co-Owner, Team Liquid 
  • Ganesh Sivakumar, Global Chief Data Analytics & AI Officer, Mead Johnson Nutrition 
  • Louise Burke, Chief Executive Officer, Open Data Institute 

Each brings different approaches, unique challenges, and proven outcomes from real-world experience across finance, telecommunications, technology, esports, and consumer goods. 

3. Gain actionable insights for your organization 

Develop a strategic perspective on the decisions that determine AI success. Join us for: 

  • Clarity on the shift: Discover why shared organizational knowledge is becoming a structural requirement for AI success. 
  • The architectural framework: Learn how a business data fabric connects data, processes, and business rules to keep AI grounded in business context. 
  • The integration model: See how technology, process, and culture must evolve together, and why all three are essential for success. 

Whether you lead data and analytics, drive business strategy, or oversee technology implementation, this event provides the insights to successfully navigate this transition. 

Save your spot 

The conversation about data and AI is everywhere, but most of it focuses on what AI can do, not what it needs to succeed. Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the leaders who are building the foundation for reliable AI. 

Register now for The Fabric of Data & AI and join the conversation on March 24, 2026.

entry.title: Re: Invoice delivery audit
entry.content: Hi Johan, Yes we want to know the available quantity and the reserved quantity the only problem is the reserved invoice doesn't reserve or commit quantity so if another sales person issues another invoice the quantity would be sold twice and we would have a short inventory Yes we keep the sold and available stock at the same place until the customer brings the receipt  While making the delivery right away might solve the problem partially but there will be a gap were another employee sells the reserved items it won't help for reports like how much did the customer take to pick his items. Regards

entry.title: Replicate the QR Code to custom Smartform, decoding from Table EDOPLINV
entry.content:
Hi All,

How to replicate the QR Code that was accepted by the KSeF and sent back to the company which is actually stored in the Table EDOPLINV-QRCODE_ACCEPTED.

I have the following test code written in the Smart form, very similar to this blog post QR Code in Base64 encoding for KSA E-Invoicing - Directly from data in Table EDOSAINV  
Note: Saudi Arabia wants the QR Code to be Base 64 encoded, but there is no such requirement for PL.

  1. Configured System Bar Code in SE73 for QR Code with "QR Code 2005".
  2. Created a smart form style, For a  Character font, assigned the QR Code system bar code it.
  3. Assigned the custom style to my custom Smart form.
  4. Below is the code written in the Initialization of Smartform (Please note this is a prototype)
SELECT SINGLE
  FROM edoplinv
FIELDS *
 WHERE edoc_guid = '699D630206330553E1000000A467BBA9'
  INTO ls_edoplinv.

  DATA lv_image_base64 TYPE string.
  DATA lo_xstr_to_str_conv TYPE REF TO cl_abap_conv_in_ce.
  cl_abap_conv_in_ce=>create(
    EXPORTING
      encoding = 'UTF-8'
      input    = ls_edoplinv-qr_code_accepted
    RECEIVING
      conv     = lo_xstr_to_str_conv
  ).
  lo_xstr_to_str_conv->read( IMPORTING data = lv_image_base64 ).

    DATA: lv_image_xstring TYPE xstring.
  lv_image_xstring = cl_http_utility=>decode_x_base64( encoded = lv_image_base64 ).

*--Assign to the QRCode variable used in the UI
EV_QRCODE = lv_image_xstring

In the smart form, created a text with the following snippet

<QR>&EV_QRCODE&</>​

The QR Code is generated, but does not point to the KSeF, instead shows a 4 digit gibberish value.

Expected URL starts with below URL
https://qr-demo.ksef.mf.gov/client-app/invoice/

 Can you please suggest how to fix it ?

entry.title: Re: Create new/custom PO Header or Item Text in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Ruby,
thanks for your question.

Short answer

Yes – in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, the recommended way to add a custom PO header or item text and make it visible in the Fiori PO apps is to use key‑user extensibility via the “Custom Fields” app (Custom Fields & Logic). You work with the Procurement: Purchasing Document and Procurement: Purchasing Document Item business contexts and then enable the field on the relevant UIs and (optionally) APIs. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
Because your PO is not printed and is only stored for reference, you do not need to touch forms/output management – you can keep everything in the Fiori and API layer.

Recommended approach (header & item text visible in PO Fiori apps)

Below is a generic pattern that works for the standard PO apps in S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

1. Create the custom field in the correct business context

  1. Log on to the SAP Fiori launchpad as an administrator / key user.
  2. Open the Custom Fields app (in the Extensibility group). [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  3. Create a new custom field:
    • Choose + (Add).
    • For a header‑level PO text, use business context
      • Procurement: Purchasing Document. [help.sap.com]
    • For an item‑level PO text, use business context
      • Procurement: Purchasing Document Item. [help.sap.com]
    • Choose a suitable field type, for example:
      • Text with appropriate length (for short notes), or
      • Long Text / Text Area if you want a longer, free‑form comment field (depending on what’s offered in your release).
  4. Maintain the basic properties (label, tooltip, length, etc.) and save the draft.
The external documentation confirms that key users can extend the purchase order apps using exactly these business contexts for header and item level. [help.sap.com]

2. Enable the custom field in the relevant PO UIs (Fiori apps)

After the field is created, you must explicitly enable it for the Fiori apps:
  1. In Custom Fields, open your newly created field again.
  2. Go to the UIs and Reports tab. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  3. Look for the PO‑related data sources, for example (names may vary slightly by release):
    • Purchase Order GUI Application (header)
    • Purchase Order Item GUI (item) [help.sap.com]
    • Plus any specific Fiori apps you use such as:
      • Manage Purchase Orders
      • Create Purchase Order – Advanced (if applicable) [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  4. Tick the checkbox to enable your field for these UIs.
  5. Publish the custom field.
According to the PO extensibility documentation, once you have enabled the custom fields for the purchase‑order‑related data sources in the UIs and Reports section, the fields appear in the Custom Fields section of the PO header and/or item details. [help.sap.com]
After publishing, users will typically see your field:
  • In the header details → “Custom Fields” or similar subsection.
  • In the item details → “Custom Fields” tab/section at item level.

3. (Optional) Enable the custom field for APIs used to replicate the PO from the external system

Because your POs are coming from an external system and only stored in S/4HANA Cloud for reference, you likely also want this custom text field to be available in the integration layer.
  1. Stay in the same custom field in the Custom Fields app.
  2. Still under UIs and Reports, look for API / data source entries, for example:
    • Purchase Order (OData V4) – this API explicitly supports extension fields added via the Custom Fields app. [help.sap.com]
  3. Enable the field for the relevant API and re‑publish.
The Purchase Order (OData V4) API documentation states that you can add extension fields to the API via the Custom Fields app and Custom Logic app (Key User Extensibility). [help.sap.com]
If you are using the asynchronous SOAP API PurchaseOrderRequest_In to create or update POs from your external system, you can review that service as well and align the mapping so that your external field (from the source system) is mapped into the new custom field in S/4HANA Cloud. [help.sap.com]

4. Use (optional) custom logic for defaults or validations

If you need additional behavior around the new text, you can use key‑user BAdIs via Custom Logic:
  1. Open the Custom Logic app. [help.sap.com]
  2. Search for PO‑related enhancement options (BAdIs) and create an implementation that:
    • Sets a default value for your text based on other header or item data, or
    • Performs checks/validations (e.g., require a comment if some condition is met).
The general extensibility guide explains that BAdIs can be implemented via Custom Logic to adapt app behavior, including additional checks or default values, and they work nicely in combination with custom fields. [help.sap.com]

5. Governance / operations tip

From a project and operations perspective, it’s a good practice to document all extensibility objects (custom fields, logic, CDS views, etc.) so you understand their impact on future releases and upgrades. This is a general recommendation for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition projects: every change to extensibility objects should be tracked and documented.
This is especially relevant in your scenario where POs are replicated from another system – it ensures everyone knows how the custom text is filled, mapped, and displayed.

Notes / constraints

  • Edition & scope: The above is valid for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition and its PO Fiori apps using the key‑user extensibility framework (Custom Fields app). [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  • No change to standard PO types needed: Adding such a field is done via extensibility. You should not need to change standard PO document type configuration to achieve this. (There is a general recommendation not to change standard PO/PR document type configuration, but to use copies if you need different behavior.)
  • Output / printing: Since you explicitly said the PO will not be printed, there is no need to enable the field on forms or email templates. If in future you decide to print, you can also enable the custom field for form templates from the same Custom Fields app. [help.sap.com]

Sources

  • Extensibility for Purchase Order Apps – business contexts and how custom fields appear in PO header/item custom fields sections [help.sap.com]
  • Custom Fields app – overview & procedure – how to create and publish custom fields, enable them for UIs/APIs/templates [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  • Purchase Order (OData V4) API – support for adding extension fields via Custom Fields & Logic [help.sap.com]
  • PurchaseOrderRequest_In (SOAP) – inbound purchase order service for external systems [help.sap.com]
  • Adaptation of App Behavior / Custom Logic app – using BAdIs for additional checks, defaulting, mappings with custom fields [help.sap.com]
  • Project recommendation: keep track of every change made to extensibility objects – general best practice for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition projects

AI transparency note

Note: This response was prepared with AI‑powered assistance like SAP Joule for Consultants (J4C) (see <https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/sap-consulting-capability.html>) and then reviewed for source support. Please validate against the linked official documentation for your specific release.
 
If this helps, please mark the answer as accepted so others can benefit as well. Thank you.

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition] Migrating from Third-...
entry.content:
Thanks for the question, Cristian.
 
Short answer:
Yes, migrating from a customer‑managed CMIS repository (e.g. OpenText via BTP DMS / CMIS) to the SAP‑managed “default” BTP Document Management Service repository is conceptually possible, but there is no one‑click or fully automated “in‑place migration” described for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition. The two guides you found both describe the technical migration of a CMIS repository to SAP DMS, not an S/4HANA‑specific end‑to‑end procedure. You’ll need to combine SAP’s generic CMIS‑to‑SAP DMS guidance with S/4HANA Cloud’s CMIS profile and repository configuration.
Below is how I’d structure the approach and how to interpret those two documents.

1. Clarify the current setup

From your description, you likely have:
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition using Attachment Service
  • Attachment Service is connected to an external CMIS‑compatible repository (OpenText) via BTP / CMIS
  • You now want to move to the SAP‑managed BTP Document Management Service (DMS) repository, which is the default option for S/4HANA Cloud, public edition.
The key is: S/4HANA Cloud does not store documents itself; it stores references (links/IDs) to objects in the CMIS repository. The migration must therefore preserve:
  • The physical documents
  • The CMIS structure and metadata that S/4HANA Cloud expects (SAP CMIS Profile)
  • The IDs / paths in such a way that S/4HANA can still resolve existing attachments after you switch repositories.

2. What the two migration guides actually cover

You mentioned:
  1. “Migrating from Third‑Party CMIS Repository” | SAP Help Portal
  2. “Migrating Third‑Party CMIS Repository to SAP Document Management Service” | SAP Help Portal
These documents are generic for BTP Document Management Service, not specific to S/4HANA Cloud, but they are the right starting point for your case. They typically describe:
  • How to export content from a third‑party CMIS repository
  • How to transform and import it into a BTP DMS repository
  • How to map folder structure, metadata and permissions in a way that SAP applications can consume
In parallel, for S/4HANA Cloud you must follow the documentation for Third‑Party Repository Integration with S/4HANA Cloud via CMIS and the SAP CMIS Profile, which explains how S/4HANA expects business objects and attachments to appear in the CMIS repository. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
So:
  • The migration guides tell you how to move CMIS content into SAP DMS.
  • The S/4HANA Cloud + CMIS docs tell you how the repository must look so that S/4HANA continues to work.
You’ll likely need both sets of documentation.

3. Recommended high‑level migration approach

A possible high‑level plan (conceptual, to be refined for your customer):
  1. Analyse existing CMIS implementation (OpenText)
    • Review how the existing repository implements the SAP CMIS Profile (folder structure, object types, properties like business object keys, content types, etc.). [help.sap.com]
    • Identify which business object types (e.g. FI documents, sales documents, purchase orders) are in scope.
  2. Provision the SAP‑managed BTP DMS repository
    • Set up a SAP Document Management Service, repository option (SAP‑managed) instance as per BTP DMS documentation.
    • Ensure it is configured with CMIS support and that it can represent the SAP CMIS Profile structures required by S/4HANA Cloud. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  3. Perform CMIS‑level content migration
    • Use the guidance in the two “Third‑Party CMIS Repository migration” documents to:
      • Export content + metadata from OpenText CMIS.
      • Map and import into the SAP DMS repository, preserving:
        • Folder hierarchy / structure expected by the SAP CMIS Profile
        • Object properties (business object keys, document types, etc.)
        • Versioning and, where possible, permissions.
    • The critical requirement is that, after migration, the new SAP DMS repository exposes the same CMIS object IDs or a consistent mapping that S/4HANA can use when resolving existing attachments. The official documentation describes how S/4HANA Cloud uses CMIS APIs to locate content in the repository; make sure your migration doesn’t break that mapping. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  4. Reconfigure S/4HANA Cloud to point to the new repository
    • In S/4HANA Cloud, update the Third‑Party Repository Integration configuration so that the Attachment Service now points to the SAP DMS repository instead of the OpenText CMIS endpoint. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
    • This step is where any mismatch of IDs or structure will surface: if the repository content is migrated 1:1 at CMIS level, S/4HANA should be able to resolve existing attachments without change.
  5. Test with sample business objects first
    • Before switching the entire landscape, test for a few representative objects:
      • Open existing FI document, sales order, purchase order, etc.
      • Check that attachments are still visible and that new uploads are stored in the SAP‑managed DMS repository.
    • If tests show broken links, you may need an additional mapping layer or a repair script at the CMIS level (this is not covered out‑of‑the‑box in generic documentation and would be a project‑specific activity).
  6. Plan cut‑over and fall‑back
    • Schedule a cut‑over window where:
      • You first freeze changes (no document uploads) in the old repository.
      • Complete the final delta migration into SAP DMS.
      • Switch S/4HANA Cloud config to the new repository.
    • Keep the old repository read‑only for a period as a fall‑back and for forensic checks.

4. Which of the two guides is the “right one”?

Because these are BTP DMS‑level guides (not S/4HANA‑Cloud‑specific), the “right” guide depends on the exact implementation, but in practice you will:
  • Use “Migrating from Third‑Party CMIS Repository” as the conceptual migration overview (how to handle CMIS export/import, structure, and metadata).
  • Use “Migrating Third‑Party CMIS Repository to SAP Document Management Service” as the concrete procedure for moving from a CMIS‑based repository to BTP Document Management Service (SAP‑managed).
In other words, they are complementary rather than mutually exclusive. The S/4HANA‑specific behavior is governed by:
  • Third‑Party Repository Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud via CMIS (Attachment Service + CMIS integration pattern), and
  • SAP CMIS Profile (how business objects are represented and consumed in CMIS repositories). [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]

5. Important constraints and caveats

Because there is no single S/4HANA Cloud “wizard” that migrates attachments between repositories, please keep in mind:
  • The migration is essentially a content + metadata migration at CMIS/BTP level.
  • Preserving or remapping CMIS IDs and business object references is critical for attachment continuity; this part is typically handled by a custom migration tool or script, not by S/4HANA itself.
  • Any customizations done in OpenText CMIS (custom properties, folder concepts, retention) need to be evaluated and mapped into SAP DMS equivalents where possible.
  • Always validate your approach in a test tenant / test BTP subaccount before doing anything in production.

6. References

  • Third‑Party Repository Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud via CMIS (Attachment Service) – SAP Help Portal [help.sap.com]
  • SAP CMIS Profile – how S/4HANA Cloud represents business objects in CMIS repositories [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  • CMIS APIs Invoked from SAP S/4HANA Cloud – which CMIS operations S/4HANA uses [help.sap.com]
  • General SAP S/4HANA Cloud Help Portal entry for latest integration docs [help.sap.com]
 
AI transparency note
Note: This draft was prepared with AI‑powered assistance like SAP Joule for Consultants (J4C) and then aligned with the official documentation references above. Please validate the final approach and details against the Help Portal for your specific S/4HANA Cloud release.
 
If this helped you, please consider marking the answer as accepted so others can find it as well.

entry.title: BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE returns success message but does not take effect.
entry.content:
I am trying to use the BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE inside MV45AFZZ and SE37 but the changes does not reflect on the documents.

Even though there was a message that the Sales Document was saved, the changes does not reflect when i check it in VA03 or SE16.

DATA: ls_order_header_in  TYPE bapisdh1,
      ls_order_header_inx TYPE bapisdh1x,
      lt_return           TYPE TABLE OF bapiret2.
CLEAR: 
     ls_order_header_in, 
     ls_order_header_inx, 
     lt_return.

IF vbak-auart = 'ZVCC'.

SELECT 
       vbeln 
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_vbfa)
  FROM vbfa
  WHERE vbelv = vbak-vbeln
    AND vbtyp_n = 'C'.

IF lt_vbfa IS NOT INITIAL.

" Some contract have related multiple sales doc
LOOP AT lt_vbfa INTO DATA(ls_vbfa).
  ls_order_header_in-incotermsv = vbak-incov.
  ls_order_header_inx-updateflag = 'U'.
  ls_order_header_inx-incotermsv = abap_true.

  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE'
    EXPORTING
      salesdocument    = ls_vbfa-vbeln
      order_header_in  = ls_order_header_in
      order_header_inx = ls_order_header_inx
    TABLES
      return           = lt_return.
ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.
ENDIF.

entry.title: Re: Component Customisation - EAMS_WDA_ORDNTF_OIF error
entry.content:
Hi Chris,
thanks for the detailed explanation and the screenshot.
From your description and the error list, two things stand out:
  • You’re in the WDA configuration for component EAMS_WDA_ORDNTF_OIF (Change Maintenance Order).
  • Even before you apply your actual change, you already see several FPM/Floorplan Manager errors, including:
    • “The name contains invalid characters, valid characters are A‑Z, 0‑9, _, and /”
    • “UIBB key is not valid (Component FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS, Config ID EAMS3_WDC_DIR_LINK_LI_CFG, Instance ID …)”
    • “Error at instantiation of component FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS with config ID EAMS3_WDC_DIR_LINK_LI_CFG”

1) First isolate / clean up the existing configuration errors

Before trying to hide Create Follow‑on Order, I’d try to verify whether these errors are:
  • Already present in the standard configuration, or
  • Introduced only when you start working with your custom configuration (copy / Z‑config).
Some checks you can run:
  1. Open the standard configuration again without any custom copy
    • If the same “UIBB key is not valid” and “invalid characters” errors already appear, then there may be an inconsistency in the delivered configuration, and that needs to be clarified first (e.g. via internal analysis or SAP channels), before doing any enhancement.
    • If the standard config is clean and the errors only show after working with your own config ID, then the issue is very likely in your custom configuration name or in a referenced UIBB key.
  2. Re‑check the IDs you entered manually
    • The error text explicitly says: “valid characters are A‑Z, 0‑9, _, and /”.
    • Double‑check that:
      • The configuration ID you are using (and any child UIBB configuration IDs) only use these characters, and
      • There is no leading/trailing blank or hidden character in the field (sometimes this can trigger “invalid name” symptoms).
  3. Verify the UIBB configuration actually exists and is active
    • For FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS with config ID EAMS3_WDC_DIR_LINK_LI_CFG, check in the FPM / Web Dynpro configuration tools that:
      • This configuration is present,
      • It is active, and
      • It is reachable from your main component configuration (no typo in the key).
If the UIBB configuration is missing or inactive, the framework will typically raise exactly the sort of “UIBB key is not valid” instantiation errors you are seeing.

2) Approaches to hide “Create Follow‑on Order” in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Your functional goal is to prevent users from creating YA02 follow‑on orders from this WDA app, because document type restrictions don’t fully protect you.
On S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in general, the recommended direction is to prefer standard configuration, authorizations, and clean‑core extensibility over deep UI framework changes. 
With that in mind, you basically have these options:
  1. Authorization / role-based approach (if there is a suitable auth check behind the action)
    • If the Create Follow‑on Order action is tied to an authorization object or business role (e.g. users who cannot create YA02 at all), that is usually the cleanest solution.
    • In that case you’d:
      • Make sure only a dedicated role can create YA02, and
      • Remove that role from users who should not see/use this capability.
  2. UI-level hiding via configuration / personalization (if exposed by the framework)
    • In some WDA / FPM applications, toolbar or menu actions can be hidden through:
      • Configuration of the toolbar schema / action schema, or
      • Switching off the corresponding UIBB / action in the FPM configuration.
    • The errors you are seeing need to be resolved first, though – otherwise your configuration is in an inconsistent state and further changes may not behave predictably.
  3. Re‑evaluate whether a different, more “cloud‑aligned” app could be used
    • SAP’s guidance for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is to:
      • Use standard processes and apps as far as possible and
      • Limit heavy UI framework modifications, especially in classic technologies, to reduce upgrade risk and rework. 
    • If there is a Fiori app or alternative process where you can control the follow‑on creation more cleanly (e.g. via restrictions and app-level authorizations), that may be easier to maintain over time.

3) Suggested next steps you could try

Given what we can see from the screenshot only:
  1. Clarify the baseline:
    • Confirm whether the standard EAMS_WDA_ORDNTF_OIF configuration is error‑free.
    • If errors exist even in the standard, resolve those first.
  2. Check your custom config IDs / keys:
    • Ensure all IDs involved in your copied configuration (main component config + any UIBBs for FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS) use only A‑Z, 0‑9, _ , / and actually exist.
    • Correct any typos or recreate the configuration if necessary.
  3. Once the config is clean, try hiding the action:
    • Look for the toolbar / action configuration entry that corresponds to Create Follow‑on Order and try setting it to “not visible” (or remove it from the schema), if the framework allows that.
